title: Itinerant antiferromagnetism in the antagonistic pair compound Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag
authors: Rafaela F. S. Penacchio, Nao Furukawa, Joanna M. Blawat, John Singleton, Zhouqi Li, Raquel A. Ribeiro, Sérgio L. Morelhão, Sergey L. Bud'ko, Paul C. Canfield, Tyler J. Slade
abstract: Low dimensional crystallographic motifs have long been associated with desirable physical properties. The confinement of electrons to low dimensions is thought to enhance quantum fluctuations and may promote correlated phenomena. Here, using the antagonistic pair concept, we add Y to the immiscible Co-Ag pair to discover Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag. This compound adopts a monoclinic $I$2/$m$ structure consisting of Y channels that are filled by one-dimensional zigzag and hexagonal Co chains, which extend along the crystallographic $b$-axis with no nearest neighbor contacts between Co and Ag atoms. Transport, magnetic, and specific heat measurements reveal that Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag orders antiferromagnetically at $T_N=14.9$ K with an effective magnetic moment $μ_{\text{eff}}$ = 1.4 $μ_{\text{B}}$/Co. Specific heat measurements show only a small entropy loss on the order of $0.1\,R\ln2$ associated with magnetic order, and magnetization isotherms, in DC fields up to 70 kOe at 1.8 K and in pulsed fields up to 600 kOe at 500 mK, indicate a small ordered moment of less than 0.2 $μ_B$/Co. Taken together, our results imply the presence of small, itinerant moments and strong fluctuations in Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag, suggesting that Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag may be a promising candidate material to investigate itinerant magnetic interactions in a quasi-one dimensional system.
summary: This paper reports on the synthesis and characterization of Y4Co3Ag, a novel compound designed to host quasi-one-dimensional Co chains. Through magnetic and transport measurements, the authors establish that the material orders antiferromagnetically at 14.9 K, exhibiting signatures of itinerant magnetism. The findings suggest Y4Co3Ag is a promising platform for investigating magnetic interactions in low-dimensional correlated electron systems.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of antiferromagnetism in the novel compound Y4Co3Ag, aiming to characterize its magnetic interactions within a quasi-one-dimensional structure.
main_result: The material exhibits antiferromagnetic ordering at $T_N=14.9$ K, suggesting the presence of small, itinerant magnetic moments and strong magnetic fluctuations in a low-dimensional Co chain system.
method: The research employed a combination of structural characterization (XRD) and multiple physical measurements, including temperature-dependent resistivity, specific heat, and high/low-field magnetization.
model_or_system: The physical system is the intermetallic compound Y4Co3Ag, which crystallizes in a monoclinic structure featuring one-dimensional Co chains running along the b-axis, designed using the 'antagonistic pair' concept to avoid Co-Ag nearest-neighbor contacts.
key_observables: Antiferromagnetic ordering temperature ($T_N$), effective magnetic moment ($\mu_{	ext{eff}}$), specific heat anomaly, and magnetization isotherms $M(H)$.
important_parameters: The system is characterized by quasi-one-dimensional Co chains, exhibiting itinerant magnetic behavior, and the magnetic coupling is analyzed via Curie-Weiss fits ($\Theta/T_N$ ratio).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the structural design successfully isolates the Co chains, allowing the study of itinerant magnetism in a quasi-1D setting. Contamination from the growth crucible ($	ext{Al}$) is also analyzed as a systematic perturbation.
figures_summary: Figures show crystal structures, temperature dependence of $\chi$, $ho$, and $C_p$ around $T_N=14.9$ K. Magnetization isotherms $M(H)$ are presented for various fields and temperatures, comparing results from different growth conditions (Ta vs. $	ext{Al}_2	ext{O}_3$).
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the material and its structure based on the antagonistic pair concept. It then presents comprehensive measurements (transport, magnetic, specific heat) confirming antiferromagnetic ordering. The analysis focuses on interpreting these results to classify the magnetism as itinerant, contrasting it with localized models, and finally discusses the sensitivity of the magnetic properties to sample growth conditions.
why_interesting: The work provides an experimental realization of designing magnetic materials based on structural motifs (antagonistic pairs) to isolate and study specific magnetic interactions, which is highly relevant for understanding correlated electron physics in low dimensions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structure of Y4Co3Ag. (a) A view of the crystal structure along the b-axis, emphasizing the complete sheathing of Co by Y atoms with no nearest-neighbor Co-Ag contacts. Both Y and Ag form linear chains running along the b-axis of the monoclinic (I2/m) unit cell. To highlight these chains, (a) shows a projection of two unit cells along the (010) direction. The intrachain distance between two Y or two Ag atoms is 3.9197(3) ˚A, i.e., the unit cell length in the (010) direction. The Co ions form both (b) hexagonal and (c) zigzag chains that occupy the channels formed by Y. Co-Co distances within the hexagonal chains are dCo1-Co1 = 2.3432(6) ˚A and dCo1-Co2 = 2.2893(6) ˚A,...

FIG. 2. Low-temperature physical properties of Y4Co3Ag. (a) Anisotropic temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibil- ity χ = M/H. The inset shows χ−1, and the solid curves are the Curie-Weiss fits (see text) to the data. (b) Temperature dependence of the electrical resistivity ρ measured with cur- rent applied parallel (black) and perpendicular (blue) to the b-axis. The right inset shows ρ(T) for T = 1.8–300 K, and the left inset shows a picture of typical rod-like Y4Co3Ag crystals on a mm-scale grid. (c) Temperature dependence of the molar heat capacity, Cp, for temperatures 1.8 ≤T ≤32 K. The in- set shows Cp/T against T 2, showing linear dependence below TN, and yielding γ = 50.1(5)...

FIG. 3. Field-dependent magnetization isotherms for Y4Co3Ag measured at (a) T = 1.8 K in a DC field and (b) 500 mK (solid curves) in a pulsed field. The points (open cir- cles) in (b) show the low-field data used to scale the pulsed- field data (see Methods).
